lessons.title: C++23 in Context
lessons.motivation: Before diving into the individual features of C++23, it helps to understand the broader context: where this standard sits in the language's history, what it fundamentally brought to the table, and who this course is for. Without that framing, individual improvements feel like an unrelated collection of details — and you lose sight of where the language is heading. This lesson is an orientation: by the end of it you will know exactly what awaits you in each of the 10 remaining modules and can plan your study accordingly.
lessons.theory:
**C++23 in the history of the standard.** C++23 was officially ratified by ISO in 2024. The key documents are the working draft N4950 (February 2023) and the final text N4958, published as ISO/IEC 14882:2024. C++23 sits between C++20 and the upcoming C++26: C++20 carried out large-scale structural reform (modules, concepts, coroutines, ranges), while C++23 is an iteration of polish and gap-filling, where each of its three flagship features solves a specific long-standing problem in the language or its library.

**The three flagship features of C++23.** The first is **`deducing this`** (P0847, explicit object parameter). Before C++23 there was no way to write a single template method that worked correctly across `const` and non-`const` qualifications and different value categories — every variant had to be duplicated. `deducing this` introduces the syntax `void foo(this Self&& self)`, where the type and category of the object are deduced automatically, dramatically reducing duplication in both library and application code. The second flagship is **`std::expected<T, E>`** (P0323) — a standard type for returning either a value or an error without exceptions or the magic of raw return codes. The third is **`std::generator<T>`** (P2502) — the first standard coroutine type: a convenient way to write lazy sequences on top of the coroutine machinery introduced in C++20.

**Who this course is for.** The course targets developers who are confident with C++17 and have a working familiarity with C++20: an understanding of concepts, ranges, `std::span`, `std::format`, and the basics of coroutines. C++14/17 is not explained here — it is assumed known. C++20 is recalled only where it is critical to understanding a specific C++23 feature. Everything from earlier standards is covered only via external links.

**Course structure.** The course consists of 11 modules and 30 lessons. Each lesson contains theory and examples; there are no exercises, and progress is recorded via a "Mark as read" button. The module order follows dependencies: context → language improvements → `deducing this` → `std::expected` → C++23 ranges → `std::print` → containers and utilities → `std::generator` → numerics and performance → removed features. This ordering ensures that each subsequent topic builds on what has already been covered.

**C++23 in practice.** Compiler support for C++23 is already broad: GCC 13+, Clang 17+, and MSVC 19.36+ cover the majority of the standard. This means that most of the features in this course are not academic exercises — they are tools you can apply in real projects today. The course focuses on practical understanding: why a feature exists, how it works, and what it looks like in code — without requiring you to compile anything yourself, but with pre-verified output examples.
